sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Jordan Roberts, is a 49-year-old male with a history of hypertension and palpitations, who presented with high blood pressure and palpitations in the emergency room in March.
He attributes his symptoms to a panic attack caused by consuming coffee and no breakfast on that day.
He denies any recurrence of symptoms since then.

He has a family history of hypertension, which he believes was getting better, but his blood pressure is still not under control despite being on three medications, including benicar HCT (hydrochlorothiazide) and amlodipine.
He has been taking his medications as prescribed and has been monitoring his blood pressure at home, which has been running at 146-155 mmHg.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of hypertension since a young age, and his family has a history of hypertension as well.
Review of systems: No other symptoms mentioned.
Current medications: Amlodipine 10 mg daily, benicar HCT 40-25 mg daily.
Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned.
Physical exam: The patient's heart and lungs sound good, and there is no swelling in his ankles and feet.
Test Results: Not mentioned.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient presents with resistant hypertension, despite being on maximum doses of his current medications.
Problem list: Hypertension; suspected resistant hypertension; suspected hyperaldosteronism.
Plan:
I have added spironolactone to his regimen to try to lower his blood pressure.
We will need to monitor his potassium and sodium levels closely.
He will have a follow-up appointment in two weeks to check his blood pressure and electrolytes.
If the new medication does not work, the next step would be to refer him to the resistant hypertension clinic.
Follow-up: The patient will be seen again in two weeks to check his blood pressure and electrolytes.
